topic: A person discovers their mentor has retired without saying goodbye
raw_output:
[story 1]

The mahogany door clicked shut with a sound far too final. Elias stood in the empty office, the scent of aged paper and faint pipe tobacco suddenly overwhelming. He hadn't expected this. He’d been expecting a final, perhaps slightly awkward, farewell, a shared glance over a stack of blueprints. Instead, silence. He ran a hand over his jaw, the movement jerky. "Right," he muttered, the sound scraping his throat. He walked to the window, staring out at the indifferent city lights, a tightness constricting his chest. He felt a sudden, sharp need to demand an explanation, to press against the closed door until the structure gave way. He straightened his tie, forcing his shoulders back, a rigid posture that betrayed the churning anxiety beneath his calm exterior.

[story 2]

"You're not leaving, Professor," Clara said, her voice pitched a little too high, a practiced attempt at nonchalance that fell flat in the quiet room. She watched him, noting the way his knuckles were white where he gripped the edge of the desk. He hadn't moved. His gaze was fixed on the framed photograph on the shelf—a picture of them on a beach trip years ago. A low growl vibrated in his chest, a sound he instantly regretted. He took a deliberate, slow breath, trying to smooth the sudden tension radiating off him. "I need to understand this," he finally managed, his tone carefully modulated, betraying none of the internal storm raging behind his eyes. He didn't turn around, unwilling to give her the satisfaction of seeing the tremor in his hands.

[story 3]

The receptionist’s cheerful greeting was a flimsy shield against the cold reality settling in Elias’s stomach. He stood by the reception desk, his hands clasped so tightly his fingers ached. He had rehearsed this conversation in his head a hundred times—a polite inquiry, a request for clarification. But the words wouldn't form. He just stared at the calendar, the date circled prominently, the next appointment long since vanished. A deep furrow settled between his brows. He shifted his weight from one foot to the other, a restless energy bubbling beneath his skin. When the receptionist asked if he needed anything, he shook his head once, sharply, the movement conveying a firm refusal to accept the presented narrative.
